backup.title: Backup
backup.icloudSyncDesc: Automatically saves a backup to your iCloud folder when you leave the app.
backup.icloudTip: To enable iCloud sync go to Settings > Your name > iCloud > Apps Using iCloud and enable HuertoTracker.
backup.exportDesc: Save the file to Files, Drive, or share it.
backup.importDesc2: Restore from a .json file. Replaces all data.
backup.pdfDesc: Summary of your garden: plants, harvests and year activity.
backup.footerNote: Backup does not include photos (stored in device gallery) or subscription status.
backup.proDesc: Automatic iCloud sync is available on the Pro plan.
backup.importDesc:
This will replace ALL current data with data from the backup file. The app will need to restart.

Continue?
backup.restoredDesc: Close and reopen the app for changes to take effect.
backup.exportError: Could not export backup. Please try again.

notifications.sowingRemindersDesc: Monthly alert on the 1st with what you can sow

notifications.noZoneWarning: Complete onboarding to activate personalised zone alerts.
notifications.frostAlertsDesc: Get a notification when temperatures below 2°C are forecast in the coming days.
notifications.frostNoProvince: Set your province in the garden settings to enable frost alerts.
notifications.transplantDesc: Alert at 6 weeks from sowing to move seedling to the bed
notifications.harvestDesc: Alert 3 days before the estimated harvest date
notifications.treatmentDesc: Notification when the waiting period after a treatment expires

notifications.harvestPushBody: Harvest expected in 3 days. Check ripeness.

recommendation.hojas: Ideal for sowing lettuces, spinach and aromatic herbs.
recommendation.raices: Perfect for carrots, radishes and root vegetables.
recommendation.flores: Transplant flowers and aromatic plants today.
recommendation.descanso: New moon day. Rest the soil and plan ahead.

gardenType.balconDesc: Growing in containers outdoors. The calendar will filter crops suitable for containers.
gardenType.macetaDesc: Growing indoors or on windowsills. The calendar will filter crops suitable for indoor growing.

entryEdit.title: Edit entry
entryEdit.deleteDesc: This entry will be permanently deleted.

tomate.name: Tomato
tomate.tips: Remove suckers regularly to focus energy on the fruits. Water at the base, never on the leaves.

pimiento.name: Pepper
pimiento.tips: Needs warm nights to set fruit well. On terraces, place it against a south-facing wall.

berenjena.name: Aubergine
berenjena.tips: The most heat-demanding crop in the nightshade family. Harvest before the skin loses its shine.

fresa.name: Strawberry
fresa.tips: Plant in autumn to harvest in spring. Mulch with straw to retain moisture and prevent fruit touching the soil.

lechuga.name: Lettuce
lechuga.tips: Stagger sowings every 2–3 weeks for a continuous supply. In heat it bolts quickly; choose heat-resistant varieties.

espinaca.name: Spinach
espinaca.tips: Cool-season crop. Harvest outer leaves and the heart will keep producing for several weeks.

acelga.name: Swiss Chard
acelga.tips: Very productive and hardy. Cut from the outside leaving the heart; one plant can last more than a year.

rucula.name: Rocket
rucula.tips: Grows in 4 weeks. Gets very peppery and bolts in heat; sow in partial shade in summer.

canonigos.name: Lamb's Lettuce
canonigos.tips: Ideal autumn-winter crop to keep the garden productive in the cold season. Sow densely.

zanahoria.name: Carrot
zanahoria.tips: Needs deep, loose soil without stones for the root to grow straight. Thin to 8 cm when seedlings are 5 cm tall.

rabano.name: Radish
rabano.tips: The fastest crop in the garden, ideal for filling gaps. Harvest promptly or it becomes woody and overly peppery.

remolacha.name: Beetroot
remolacha.tips: Each seed is a fruit containing several seeds; thin to 10 cm leaving the most vigorous seedling.

patata.name: Potato
patata.tips: Earth up soil around the stem as it grows for more tubers. Harvest when the plant yellows.

nabo.name: Turnip
nabo.tips: Rustic autumn crop. Harvest small (5–7 cm) for best flavour; larger ones become bitter and fibrous.

judia-verde.name: Green Bean
judia-verde.tips: Harvest frequently while pods still bend; letting them mature exhausts the plant. Climbing varieties need 1.5 m support.

guisante.name: Pea
guisante.tips: Cool-season crop. Provide support from the start. Harvest when pods are well-filled but still green.

haba.name: Broad Bean
haba.tips: Sow in autumn for greater yield. Pinch the main stem when first fruits appear to control black aphid.

brocoli.name: Broccoli
brocoli.tips: Harvest the central head before it flowers. Plants will keep producing small side shoots for weeks.

coliflor.name: Cauliflower
coliflor.tips: When the curd forms, fold the outer leaves over it to blanch and protect it from the sun.

col.name: Cabbage
col.tips: Very frost-hardy. Plant in firm soil and earth up around the stem so it doesn't topple in the wind.

kale.name: Kale
kale.tips: Harvest lower and outer leaves; the plant keeps growing from the top. Cold improves its flavour, making it sweeter.

calabacin.name: Courgette
calabacin.tips: A single plant can yield 3–4 kg per week. Harvest at 15–20 cm so the plant doesn't exhaust itself.

pepino.name: Cucumber
pepino.tips: Train on a trellis to save space on terraces. Harvest daily when production is at its peak.

calabaza.name: Pumpkin
calabaza.tips: Needs lots of space; on small terraces use small-fruited or climbing varieties. Ready when the stem dries.

melon.name: Melon
melon.tips: Prune to 2–3 fruits per plant for large, sweet melons. Reduce watering once fruits have set to concentrate sugar.

sandia.name: Watermelon
sandia.tips: Tap the fruit gently: a hollow sound means ripe. The tendril closest to the fruit should be dry at harvest.

albahaca.name: Basil
albahaca.tips: Pinch to encourage branching and prevent bolting. The best tomato companion: repels whitefly and aphids.

perejil.name: Parsley
perejil.tips: Slow to germinate (3–4 weeks). Soak seeds 24 h before sowing to speed it up. Cut from the outside, leaving the heart.

cilantro.name: Coriander
cilantro.tips: Bolts quickly in heat; sow in partial shade or in staggered sowings every 3 weeks for continuous production.

romero.name: Rosemary
romero.tips: Hardy perennial. Better to propagate by cuttings in spring than from seed. Does not tolerate waterlogging.

tomillo.name: Thyme
tomillo.tips: Mediterranean herb that withstands drought well. Prune after flowering to keep it compact and productive.

menta.name: Mint
menta.tips: Very invasive: always plant in a pot or with an underground barrier. Prefers partial shade in summer.

ajo.name: Garlic
ajo.tips: Plant individual cloves tip-up, 5 cm deep. When half the leaves dry out, it's time to harvest.

cebolla.name: Onion
cebolla.tips: Bend the leaves when they fall naturally and leave the bulb to cure in the ground for 2 weeks. Store cool and dry.

puerro.name: Leek
puerro.tips: Transplant into deep holes and earth up gradually to blanch the stem. Stays in the ground all autumn-winter; harvest as needed.

guindilla.name: Chilli / Chile
guindilla.tips: Controlled water stress increases capsaicin and heat. Harvest green or let ripen to red for maximum flavour.

apio.name: Celery
apio.tips: Needs constant moisture or stalks turn fibrous and bitter. Earth up around the stems to blanch and soften them.

esparrago.name: Asparagus
esparrago.tips: Perennial crop: do not harvest until year 3 so roots establish well. Then produces for 15–20 years with minimal effort.

salvia.name: Sage
salvia.tips: Mediterranean perennial. Its flowers attract pollinators. Cut back after flowering to keep it compact and productive season after season.

oregano.name: Oregano
oregano.tips: Heat and drought intensify the flavour. Cut before it flowers for maximum aroma. Dry bunches hanging upside down.

eneldo.name: Dill
eneldo.tips: Bolts quickly in summer; sow in succession every 3 weeks. Never plant next to fennel — they cross-pollinate and lose their characteristic flavour.

hinojo.name: Fennel
hinojo.tips: Suppresses growth of almost everything nearby. Plant in an isolated corner or a separate pot to enjoy its benefits without harming the garden.

manzanilla.name: Chamomile
manzanilla.tips: Harvest flowers at full opening for highest oil concentration. Excellent companion that repels harmful insects and attracts pollinators.

lavanda.name: Lavender
lavanda.tips: Drought-tolerant perennial. Prune after flowering to prevent it becoming woody. Excellent for repelling aphids and moths.

identify.title: Identify pest
identify.subtitle: Photograph the affected part of your plant
identify.healthyDesc: No pests or diseases were detected
identify.proDesc: Photograph the affected area and the AI instantly identifies pests, diseases, and nutritional deficiencies.
identify.errorDesc: Could not analyze the image. Please try again.

plantFaq.errorDesc: Could not generate questions. Please try again.
plantFaq.aiNote: Answers generated by AI · verify with an agronomic expert

plantScan.title: Identify Plant
plantScan.subtitle: Photograph your plant to identify it
plantScan.notIdentifiedDesc: Could not recognise the plant. Select it manually.
plantScan.errorDesc: Could not analyse the image. Please try again.
plantScan.proDesc: Photograph your plant and AI identifies it automatically.

atlantica.1: Make the most of dry days to till and prepare the soil.
atlantica.2: Sow potatoes and cabbages outdoors once soil temperature exceeds 5°C.
atlantica.3: Time to transplant tomato and pepper seedlings under cover.
atlantica.4: Take advantage of the rain to plant green beans and peas.
atlantica.5: Sow courgette and cucumber outdoors; look after the pollinators.
atlantica.6: Water at dusk to reduce evaporation; protect fruit from excess moisture.
atlantica.7: Harvest tomatoes and peppers before heavy rain arrives.
atlantica.8: Peak harvest month: pick regularly to encourage more production.
atlantica.9: Sow spinach, Swiss chard and autumn lettuces.
atlantica.10: Plant garlic and winter onions before the first frost.
atlantica.11: Work compost into the soil to improve structure for next year.
atlantica.12: Plan next season's garden and order seeds from catalogues.

mediterranea.1: Start pepper and aubergine seedlings indoors; frost risk still present outside.
mediterranea.2: Transplant strawberries and plant broad beans; cool weather suits them.
mediterranea.3: Sow tomatoes under cover; transplant lettuces and cabbages outdoors.
mediterranea.4: Move tomatoes and peppers outside once night temperatures stay above 10°C.
mediterranea.5: Install drip irrigation before the heat; sow green beans and courgette.
mediterranea.6: Harvest garlic and onions; mulch to conserve soil moisture.
mediterranea.7: Peak tomato production: harvest daily to encourage new fruit.
mediterranea.8: Sow summer beans and prepare autumn beds in shadier spots.
mediterranea.9: Sow Swiss chard, spinach and autumn lettuces; heat is easing.
mediterranea.10: Plant garlic and onions; prepare beds with compost for winter.
mediterranea.11: Transplant broccoli and cauliflower; autumn rain helps them establish.
mediterranea.12: Protect frost-sensitive plants and plan crop rotation.

continental.1: Start seedlings indoors; cold still prevents outdoor sowing.
continental.2: Begin tomato, pepper and aubergine seedlings under cover.
continental.3: Transplant lettuces and peas; wait until frost risk has passed.
continental.4: Sow beans and Swiss chard outdoors; transplant tomatoes only if frost-free.
continental.5: Water regularly: nights are still cool but days can be warm.
continental.6: Harvest early garlic and onions; maintain irrigation during dry spells.
continental.7: Harvest tomatoes and cucurbits; increase watering on days above 35°C.
continental.8: Sow spinach and late lettuces; intensive summer harvest.
continental.9: Heat is easing: sow cabbages, broccoli and Swiss chard.
continental.10: Plant winter garlic; clear beds and apply compost.
continental.11: Late cabbage harvest; cover with fleece if frost threatens.
continental.12: Protect the most tender plants; plan next year's rotation.

subtropical.1: Sow tomatoes, peppers and cucumbers: temperatures are ideal.
subtropical.2: Plant strawberries and sow lettuces; mild weather aids establishment.
subtropical.3: Transplant cucurbits; make the most of any spring rains.
subtropical.4: Increase watering as temperatures rise; mulch to retain moisture.
subtropical.5: Shade crops from excess sun with a shade net.
subtropical.6: Rainy season: water moderately and watch for fungal disease.
subtropical.7: Peak rainfall: good drainage is key; harvest before rot sets in.
subtropical.8: Sow beans and sweet corn; warm nights encourage germination.
subtropical.9: Late summer harvest; sow lettuces and spinach in the shade.
subtropical.10: Cool weather: ideal for brassicas, carrots and beetroot.
subtropical.11: Sow cabbages and broccoli; cool temperatures improve flavour.
subtropical.12: Harvest and plan: summer crops are finishing their cycle.

costs.title: Costs & ROI
costs.proDesc: Track your season expenses, calculate your garden's ROI, and follow the value of each harvest.

mildiu.name: Downy Mildew
mildiu.symptoms: Yellow spots on the upper leaf surface, grey-purple mould on the underside. Leaves dry out and drop.
mildiu.description: Fungal disease caused by Peronospora spp. Favoured by high humidity and cool temperatures.

treatments.name: Bordeaux mixture
treatments.instructions: Apply at first symptoms. Repeat every 7–10 days in wet weather.

treatments.name: Improve ventilation
treatments.instructions: Increase plant spacing and water at the base, never overhead.

treatments.name: Copper fungicide
treatments.instructions: Apply as per label. Avoid during flowering to protect pollinators.

oidio.name: Powdery Mildew
oidio.symptoms: White powdery coating on leaves, stems and fruit. Affected leaves curl and dry out.
oidio.description: Superficial fungus (Erysiphe spp.) that thrives in warm days and cool nights with high ambient humidity.

treatments.name: Diluted milk
treatments.instructions: Mix 1 part milk in 9 parts water and spray weekly.

treatments.name: Baking soda
treatments.instructions: 5 g per litre of water + a few drops of dish soap. Spray every 7 days.

treatments.name: Wettable sulphur
treatments.instructions: Apply early morning. Do not use when temperatures exceed 30 °C.

botrytis.name: Botrytis (Grey Mould)
botrytis.symptoms: Soft rot covered with grey-brown mould. Affects stems, leaves and fruit in humid conditions.
botrytis.description: Botrytis cinerea is one of the most common fungi in vegetable gardens. Very active in spring–autumn with high humidity.

treatments.name: Prune affected parts
treatments.instructions: Remove and destroy (do not compost) infected tissue immediately.

treatments.name: Trichoderma
treatments.instructions: Apply Trichoderma harzianum biofungicide preventively.

treatments.name: Systemic fungicide
treatments.instructions: Rotate active ingredients to avoid resistance.

pulgon.name: Aphids
pulgon.symptoms: Colonies of small insects (green, black or grey) on new growth and leaf undersides. Distorted leaves and sticky honeydew.
pulgon.description: Aphids suck sap, weakening the plant and spreading viruses. Attracted by excess nitrogen.

treatments.name: Potassium soap
treatments.instructions: Spray directly on colonies, thoroughly wetting the leaf underside. Repeat 2–3 times, 5 days apart.

treatments.name: Repellent plants
treatments.instructions: Plant basil, lavender or mint nearby to repel aphids.

treatments.name: Neem oil
treatments.instructions: Mix 5 ml emulsified neem oil + 2 ml dish soap per litre. Spray at dusk.

mosca_blanca.name: Whitefly
mosca_blanca.symptoms: Clouds of tiny white flies when plants are disturbed. Leaves coated with honeydew and sooty black mould.
mosca_blanca.description: Trialeurodes vaporariorum. Adults and larvae suck sap. Rapid summer cycle, very difficult to eradicate.

treatments.name: Yellow sticky traps
treatments.instructions: Place at foliage height. Replace when full.

treatments.name: Soap + neem oil mix
treatments.instructions: Synergistic spray applied at dusk to avoid harming beneficial insects.

treatments.name: Encarsia formosa
treatments.instructions: Natural parasite. Introduce in greenhouse following supplier instructions.

araña_roja.name: Spider Mites
araña_roja.symptoms: Yellow stippling on leaves, fine webbing on the underside. Bronzed, prematurely falling leaves.
araña_roja.description: Tetranychus urticae. Very active in hot, dry summer conditions. Multiplies rapidly.

treatments.name: Increase humidity
treatments.instructions: Mist the surrounding air at dusk. Avoid wetting the leaves directly.

treatments.name: Neem oil
treatments.instructions: Apply to leaf undersides where mites live. Repeat every 5–7 days.

treatments.name: Phytoseiulus persimilis
treatments.instructions: Predatory mite. Effective in greenhouses above 20 °C.

trips.name: Thrips
trips.symptoms: Silver or bronze patches on leaves and fruit. Tiny scraping marks. Spreads Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus.
trips.description: Frankliniella occidentalis. Very hard to control once established. Prefers warm, dry conditions.

treatments.name: Blue sticky traps
treatments.instructions: Monitor to detect infestations early.

treatments.name: Spinosad
treatments.instructions: Natural-origin product, approved for organic production. Observe pre-harvest intervals.

treatments.name: Amblyseius cucumeris
treatments.instructions: Predatory mite for thrips. Introduce preventively or at first sign of adults.

clorosis_ferrica.name: Iron Chlorosis
clorosis_ferrica.symptoms: Yellowing between veins on young leaves while the veins remain green.
clorosis_ferrica.description: Insufficient available iron, usually caused by high substrate pH or excess lime in irrigation water.

treatments.name: Iron chelate
treatments.instructions: Apply to substrate or as foliar spray (0.5 g/L). Repeat after 2 weeks if needed.

treatments.name: Acidify irrigation water
treatments.instructions: Add a few drops of vinegar or citric acid to lower pH to 6–6.5.

treatments.name: Compost
treatments.instructions: Organic matter improves micronutrient availability in the soil.

carencia_calcio.name: Calcium Deficiency
carencia_calcio.symptoms: Blossom end rot in tomatoes and peppers. Brown inner leaf edges in lettuce (tip burn).
carencia_calcio.description: Apical necrosis is not a lack of soil calcium but a transport failure caused by irregular watering or excess potassium.

treatments.name: Regular watering
treatments.instructions: Keep substrate evenly moist, avoiding drought and waterlogging cycles.

treatments.name: Calcium foliar spray
treatments.instructions: Spray diluted calcium nitrate (0.2%) directly on affected fruit.

treatments.name: Mulching
treatments.instructions: Mulch reduces evaporation and stabilises soil moisture.

zoneDescription.atlantica: Cool summers, mild winters, abundant rain year-round
zoneDescription.continental: Hot summers, cold winters, sparse rainfall

customCrop.emptyDesc: Create a crop that is not in the list.
customCrop.name: Name
customCrop.deleteMsg: Plants using this crop will not be deleted.
